title: [InstCombine] Preserve multi-dimensional array structure in GEP optimization
author: akadutta
description:
These changes are needed to improve register pressure on AMDGPU backends.  Commits 055bfc0 and 8a09adc simplified multi-dimensional array GEP operations into chains of single-index GEPs and stripped leading zeros. It causes significant performance drop on AMDGPUs by removing semantic information that the backend relies on. For example, multi-dimensional GEPs allow coalesced LDS reads that reduces register pressure. 

If we use the example in https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/170477, flattened GEPs lead to a pattern of ds_read->spill to scratch. If we guard the GEP flattening for multi-dimensional arrays, it allows the backend to generate code such that ds_reads are immediately used.

``````````diff
diff --git a/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstructionCombining.cpp b/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstructionCombining.cpp
index 548cf2be6..1ec37492a 100644
--- a/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstructionCombining.cpp
+++ b/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstructionCombining.cpp
@@ -3388,10 +3388,10 @@ Instruction *InstCombinerImpl::visitGetElementPtrInst(GetElementPtrInst &GEP) {
       gep_type_iterator GTI = gep_type_begin(GEP);
       ++GTI;
       if (!GTI.isStruct())
-        return replaceInstUsesWith(GEP, Builder.CreateGEP(GTI.getIndexedType(),
-                                                          GEP.getPointerOperand(),
-                                                          drop_begin(Indices), "",
-                                                          GEP.getNoWrapFlags()));
+        return replaceInstUsesWith(
+            GEP,
+            Builder.CreateGEP(GTI.getIndexedType(), GEP.getPointerOperand(),
+                              drop_begin(Indices), "", GEP.getNoWrapFlags()));
     }
   }
 

``````````

comments.author: akadutta
comments.body:
I am working off a slightly smaller version of the example in https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/170477. 
Knowing the full layout enables the backend to recognize consecutive elements in shared memory. The full layout allows the backend to do more efficient loads. Without this patch, stride calculations are lost, without which, the backend cannot optimize/coalesce vector loads. It has to generate conservative code. Consider these two ASM snippets below:

Without patch:

```
ds_read_b128 v[0:3], v1 offset:12400
s_waitcnt lgkmcnt(0)
buffer_store_dword v0, off, s[0:3], 0 offset:252 ; 4-byte Folded Spill
s_nop 0
buffer_store_dword v1, off, s[0:3], 0 offset:256 ; 4-byte Folded Spill
buffer_store_dword v2, off, s[0:3], 0 offset:260 ; 4-byte Folded Spill
buffer_store_dword v3, off, s[0:3], 0 offset:264 ; 4-byte Folded Spill
.............................................
............................................
buffer_load_dword v44, off, s[0:3], 0 offset:252 ; 4-byte Folded Reload
buffer_load_dword v45, off, s[0:3], 0 offset:256 ; 4-byte Folded Reload
buffer_load_dword v46, off, s[0:3], 0 offset:260 ; 4-byte Folded Reload
buffer_load_dword v47, off, s[0:3], 0 offset:264 ; 4-byte Folded Reload
s_waitcnt vmcnt(2)
v_fmac_f64_e32 v[40:41], v[44:45], v[0:1]
s_waitcnt vmcnt(0)
v_fmac_f64_e32 v[40:41], v[46:47], v[2:3]
```

With patch:

```
ds_read_b128 v[24:27], v58 offset:12400
s_waitcnt lgkmcnt(0)
v_fmac_f64_e32 v[50:51], v[24:25], v[8:9]
v_fmac_f64_e32 v[50:51], v[26:27], v[10:11]
```

With flattened accesses, LDS reads are immediately spilled to scratch, and then re-read just before their use. With structured accesses, the scheduler can batch multiple LDS reads together and schedule uses immediately after reads. 

Steps to repro:
opt -O3 -march=amdgcn -mcpu=gfx90a -S minimal_vgpr_spill_reproducer.ll -o opted_repro.ll
llc -march=amdgcn -mcpu=gfx90a opted_repro.ll -o opted_repro.s

[minimal_vgpr_spill_reproducer.ll.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24676329/minimal_vgpr_spill_reproducer.ll.txt)

comments.author: nikic
comments.body: I've checked the test case, and I think the root cause here is actually additional unrolling. I haven't tried figuring out what exactly changes in the cost model. I see one invariant GEP index being hoisted out now, possibly that's enough to push it over the edge?

comments.author: akadutta
comments.body:
Thank you for taking the time to look at the test case. It is indeed the case that disabling unrolling improves register pressure and removes any spills. 
However, I think the performance loss is still related to the loss of GEP structure. Simply disabling unrolling did not recover performance. Here's why I think so:
The flattened GEPs do not preserve semantic information about multi dimensional arrays. The unroller sees simpler flattened structure and unrolls more aggressively. The unroller incorrectly computes the "simplicity" of the flattened GEPs. However, when structure is preserved the unroller is more conservative. Additionally, when we do not preserve the GEP structures, it makes it difficult for the backend to recognize patterns. In the flattened case, the backend sees arbitrary offsets, falls back to simple loads, and the unrolled code leads to huge register pressure. However, when structure is preserved, the backend can recognize strided access patterns easier, can issue specialized LDS instructions, and performs better scheduling. I think that, although the unroller aggravates the problem, the cause still traces back to the GEP changes. 
Please let me know what you think.

comments.author: arsenm
comments.body:
> The flattened GEPs do not preserve semantic information about multi dimensional arrays.

The GEP type doesn't really contain semantic information about the array. It's just a size factor in the address computation

> the backend can recognize strided access patterns easier,

Can you be more specific about what code this is?
